Cybercrime incident type: Extortion or harassment involving images or videos

Cybercrime subcategory: Extortion or harassment involving images or videos
Cybercrime incident type: Respondent was threatened with the release of sensitive, personal, compromising or intimate photos, video or information that was stored online, on a digital device or sent in messages

Cybercrime subcategory: Extortion or harassment involving images or videos
Cybercrime incident type: Someone used coercion, blackmail or demands to try and get respondent to send them sensitive, personal or compromising photos, video or information that was stored online, on a digital device or sent in messages

Cybercrime subcategory: Extortion or harassment involving images or videos
Cybercrime incident type: Someone sent or posted photos and videos of respondent to others to try and embarrass, hurt or blackmail them

Cybercrime subcategory: Extortion or harassment involving images or videos
Cybercrime incident type: Someone shared or published sensitive, personal, compromising or intimate photos or videos of respondent without their consent
Impersonation
Someone hacked into respondent’s social media or network account (including communicating with their contacts or posting messages or status updates from their accounts)
Someone set up fake social media or networking profiles pretending to be respondent (eg and communicated with their contacts or posted messages or status updates from their accounts)
Someone created fake videos or photos of respondent (eg ‘deep fakes’)
Sharing without consent
Someone stole respondent’s online personal information (including photos and videos) and used it without their permission
Someone published identifying information (such as respondent’s full name, contact number, address, school etc) with malicious intent (ie doxxing)
Stalking and harassing
Someone used technology to stalk or repeatedly harass respondent, including being contacted by someone they had blocked or asked to not contact them
Controlling and restricting
Someone restricted respondent’s access to online resources (eg social media, electronic legal documents, banking and utility accounts, etc)
Someone monitored respondent’s activity online or on their phone (including installing spyware, going through their private messages, etc)
Someone tried to stop respondent from communicating with others online or over their mobile
Hate speech
Someone subjected respondent to hate speech or made derogatory, malicious or threatening comments directly to respondent based on their religion, ethnicity, gender, sexuality or ideology
Cyberbullying
Someone sent or posted mean or hurtful messages via electronic communication (eg emails, social media or text messages) that made respondent feel hurt, embarrassed or unsafe
Someone spread rumours about respondent via electronic communication (eg emails, social media or text messages)
Unsolicited sexual content
Respondent was sent unsolicited sexually explicit messages, images or videos
Respondent fell victim to some other type of online abuse and harassment, not specified above
Suspected malware
Popup ads started popping up everywhere
Respondent’s device slowed down and acted strangely
People respondent knew told them they had been sending suspicious messages and links over social media or email
Respondent’s devices kept crashing for some reason
Respondent’s browser kept getting redirected when they tried to search for a familiar site
Programs were opening and closing automatically
There was a lack of storage space that respondent couldn’t explain
Respondent’s device was working excessively while no programs were running
Respondent’s files had gone missing or been replaced with odd file extensions and the icons for the files were blank
Previously accessible system tools (such as personalised or security settings) were disabled
Pure ransomware
Respondent’s devices, servers, service or networks were disrupted (eg slowed down, lost connection, had outages) and they received instructions for paying a ransom to restore functionality
Respondent’s systems, devices or files had a virus or were inaccessible (eg locked or unreadable) and they received instructions for paying a ransom to restore access
Ransomware-related data theft and extortion
Respondent received a ransom message on their device to say their data or information had been stolen and they had to pay to prevent this information from being leaked or sold online
Other malware
Respondent fell victim to some other type of malware, not specified above
Compromise of financial accounts
Suspicious transactions appeared in respondent’s bank statements or accounts, credit card or credit report
Respondent received calls from debt collectors asking about unpaid bills they did not recognise
Someone used respondent’s details to purchase or order something or they received unfamiliar bills, invoices or receipts
Respondent was unsuccessful in applying for credit, and this was surprising given their credit history
Someone tried to open a new bank account, apply for a new loan or obtain credit with respondent’s personal details or they received credit/payment cards in the mail that they did not apply for
Someone tried to obtain money from one of respondent’s investments or superannuation accounts
Other identity compromise
Someone used respondent’s personal details (including images) to create an impersonation account to extort their contacts

Someone used respondent’s personal details to open a mobile phone or utility account, or their current mobile phone or other utility lost service because their service has been transferred to a new unknown device
Respondent got a medical bill for a service they did not receive, or a medical claim was rejected because they had unexpectedly already reached their benefits limit
Someone gained access to respondent’s cryptocurrency wallet or exchange account and made transactions or stole currency
Someone used respondent’s personal details to create a fake cryptocurrency wallet or exchange account
Someone used respondent’s personal details to attempt to apply for a job or rent a property
Someone used respondent’s personal details to fraudulently apply for government benefits
Someone used respondent’s personal details to attempt to give false info to police
Respondent was unable to file taxes because someone had already filed a tax return in their name
Respondent fell victim to some other type of identity crime and misuse, not specified above
Investment scams
Respondent lost cryptocurrency in an exit scam or ‘rug-pull’, where cryptocurrency developers or promoters abandon a project and disappear with investors’ funds
Respondent lost money or provided sensitive information in another kind of cryptocurrency scam
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer to buy into an illegitimate investment, trading or shares scheme or to get early access to their super fund
Respondent lost cryptocurrency to a scammer in a pretend ‘give away’, business opportunity or investment opportunity
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to some other fraudulent scheme related to their super fund
Job and employment scams
Respondent lost money or provided sensitive information to a scammer offering a job or employment
Buying products or services scams
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information when trying to buy a product or service from a fake or fraudulent seller online
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information when trying to sell a product or service to a fake or fraudulent buyer online
Respondent paid a fake invoice or bill for some other product or service that they did not receive
Respondent paid money for health products, medicines or drugs from an online pharmacy and the products never arrived or were counterfeit
Respondent sent money to a scammer posing as a known business supplier, service institution or company telling them that their banking details had changed
Respondent paid a fake invoice for directory listings, advertising, domain name renewals or office supplies
Donation scams
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer pretending to be a charity or disaster relief effort
Relationship scams
Respondent paid money, provided sensitive information or sent intimate images or videos to a scammer pretending to be a potential romantic partner
Remote access scams
Respondent allowed someone pretending to be from a telecommunications or computer company to remotely access their computer, or paid them money or provided sensitive information
Respondent allowed some other kind of scammer to remotely access their computer
Money recovery scam
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer who was offering to help them recover from a previous scam
Unexpected money scams
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer offering them the false promise of an inheritance or share in a large sum of money in exchange for assistance
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer offering the false promise of prize money or a holiday package
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer falsely offering a rebate from the government, a bank or trusted organisation
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer falsely offering some other benefit or payment from the government, a bank or trusted organisation
Phishing scams
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer pretending to be from a known service institution or company (bank, internet provider, post office, etc)
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to a scammer pretending to be someone they personally know, such as a family member, friend or work associate

Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to someone pretending to be a bank, government or law enforcement official for things like a speeding fine, tax office debt, or immigration or visa issue
Respondent paid money or provided sensitive information to someone pretending to be a bank, government or law enforcement official for some other fine, bill or debt
Other scams
Respondent lost money buying sports betting prediction software, or becoming a member of a sport betting syndicate or investment scheme, because these schemes did not work as advertised
Respondent paid for extremely high call or text rates when replying to unsolicited SMS competitions
Respondent sent money or provided sensitive information to some other kind of scammer who gave them fraudulent bank or payment details
Respondent fell victim to some other type of online scam or fraud, not already specified
